snippets.title: Dynamic vs strong typing
snippets.code:
x = 42
print(type(x))        # <class 'int'>

x = "a string now"    # dynamic typing: same name, new type
print(type(x))        # <class 'str'>

# Strong typing: no silent coercion
try:
    result = "3" + 5
except TypeError as e:
    print(e)          # can only concatenate str (not "int") to str
snippets.explanation: A name can be rebound to any type (dynamic), but Python refuses to mix incompatible types (strong).

snippets.title: Mutability and shared references
snippets.code:
a = [1, 2]
b = a               # same object
b.append(3)
print(a)            # [1, 2, 3] -- both names see the change

c = a.copy()        # independent shallow copy
c.append(4)
print(a)            # [1, 2, 3] -- a is unaffected
snippets.explanation: Assignment copies the reference, not the object. Use .copy() (or copy.deepcopy for nested data) to avoid surprises.

snippets.title: Type checks and type hints
snippets.code:
value = 0.7
print(isinstance(value, float))   # True -- prefer this over type() ==
print(isinstance(True, int))      # True -- bool is a subclass of int

def trim(prompt: str, max_chars: int = 100) -> str:
    """Hints document intent; they are NOT enforced at runtime."""
    return prompt[:max_chars]

print(trim("hello world", 5))     # 'hello'
snippets.explanation: isinstance respects inheritance; type hints are informational and checked by tools like mypy, not by the interpreter.

snippets.title: Typed values in a real LLM request
snippets.code:
request = {
    "model": "gpt-4o",          # str
    "temperature": 0.7,          # float, not the int 0 or 1
    "max_tokens": 512,           # int, not the string "512"
    "stream": False,             # bool
    "messages": [                # list[dict]
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are concise."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Define a variable."},
    ],
}

assert isinstance(request["max_tokens"], int)
assert isinstance(request["temperature"], float)
snippets.explanation: Every field of an LLM call is an ordinary Python value. Getting the types right (int vs str, float vs int) is what makes the request valid.
